title: Chronic social stress reduces dendritic arbors in CA3 of hippocampus and decreases binding to serotonin transporter sites
abstract:
Chronic Social Stress Reduces Dendritic
Arbors in CA3 Of Hippocampus and
Decreases Binding to Serotonin
Transporter Sites
CHRISTINA R. MCKITTRICK,1* ANA MARI´A MAGARIN˜ OS,1 D. CAROLINE BLANCHARD,2
ROBERT J. BLANCHARD,3 BRUCE S. MCEWEN,1 AND RANDALL R. SAKAI4
1Laboratory of Neuroendocrinology, The Rockefeller University, New York, New York 10021
2Department of Anatomy and Reproductive Biology, University of Hawaii at Manoa, Honolulu, Hawaii 96822
3Department of Psychology, University of Hawaii at Manoa, Honolulu, Hawaii 96822
4Department of Animal Biology, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19104
KEY WORDS
Ammon’s horn; chronic stress; Golgi impregnation; social stress; HPA
axis
ABSTRACT
Male rats housed in mixed-sex groups in a visible burrow system (VBS)
form a dominance hierarchy in which subordinate animals show stress-related changes
in behavior, endocrine function and neurochemistry. Dominants also appear to be
moderately stressed compared to controls, although these animals do not develop the
more pronounced behavioral and physiological deﬁcits seen in the subordinates. In the
present study, we examined the effects of chronic psychosocial stress on the morphology
of Golgi-impregnated CA3 pyramidal neurons. In addition, since serotonin has been
implicated in the mechanisms mediating the dendritic remodeling seen with other
chronic stress regimens, we used quantitative autoradiography to measure binding to
the serotonin transporter (5HTT) in hippocampus and dorsal and median raphe. Chronic
social stress led to a decrease in the number of branch points and total dendritic length
in the apical dendritic trees of CA3 pyramidal neurons in dominant animals compared
to unstressed controls; subordinates also had a decreased number of dendritic branch
points. [3H]paroxetine binding to the 5HTT was decreased in Ammon’s horn in both
dominants and subordinates compared to controls, while 5HTT binding remained un-
changed in dentate gyrus and raphe. The similarity of the changes in 5HTT binding and
dendritic arborization between both groups of VBS animals, despite apparent differ-
ences in stressor severity, suggests that these changes may be part of the normal
adaptive response to chronic social stress. The mechanisms underlying dendritic remod-
eling in CA3 pyramidal neurons are likely to involve stress-induced changes in glucocor-
ticoids and in 5HT and other transmitters. Synapse 36:85–94, 2000.
© 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
INTRODUCTION
Chronic stress has been shown to have diverse ef-
fects on the central nervous system, including changes
in cellular activity, neurochemistry, and neuronal mor-
phology (for review see McEwen and Mendelson, 1993).
In hippocampus, chronic stress has been shown to re-
duce the arbor complexity and total dendritic length of
the apical dendritic trees of CA3 pyramidal neurons.
This is a highly reproducible phenomenon, as several
studies have shown the remodeling to occur in rats in
response to repeated restraint and chronic multiple
stress (Magarin˜os and McEwen, 1995a,b; Sunanda et
al.1995; Watanabe et al.1992a–c) and in subordinate
tree shrews following chronic psychosocial stress (Ma-
garin˜os et al., 1996).
The ability of the serotonin (5HT) reuptake en-
hancer, tianeptine, to block the morphological changes
in the hippocampus induced by repeated restraint pro-
vides evidence for a key role of 5HT in the dendritic
shrinkage or atrophy in CA3 pyramidal neurons (Wa-
Contract grant sponsor: NSF; Contract grant number: IBN-9815480; Contract
grant sponsor: NIH; Contract grant numbers: MH41256, GM07524; Contract
grant sponsor: Harry Frank Guggenheim Foundation.
*Correspondence to: Christina R. McKittrick, Ph.D., Center for Molecular and
Behavioral Neuroscience, Rutgers University, 197 University Avenue, Newark,
NJ 07102.
E-mail: mckittrick@axon.rutgers.eduAccepted in 15 May 1999
SYNAPSE 36:85–94 (2000)
© 2000 WILEY-LISS, INC.
 tanabe et al., 1992b). The stimulatory effects of stress
on central 5HT synthesis and metabolism are well doc-
umented (reviewed in Chaouloff, 1993; McKittrick and
McEwen, 1996), while several recent studies using in
vivo techniques such as microdialysis and voltammetry
have demonstrated increased 5HT release in various
terminal ﬁelds, including hippocampus, following dif-
ferent stressors (Joseph and Kennett, 1983; Kirby et
al., 1997; Pei et al., 1990; Vahabzadeh and Fillenz,
1994). Extracellular levels of 5HT can also be regulated
by changes in the rate of clearance of synaptically
released 5HT, which is mediated by the 5HT trans-
porter. Evidence for regulation of the presynaptic 5HT
transporter (5HTT) by stress is equivocal, however.
Although acute immobilization led to a rapid decrease
in 5HTT mRNA levels in the dorsomedial hypotha-
lamic nucleus, no changes were observed in the mid-
brain raphe nuclei (Hoffman et al., 1998), and others
have found that binding to the transporter was simi-
larly unchanged by chronic restraint (Watanabe et al.,
1993).
It is currently unclear whether changes in dendritic
morphology and serotonin function are part of the nor-
mal adaptive process in response to stress. Indeed,
5HT and 5HT receptors have been implicated in mech-
anisms of adaptation to chronic stress (Kennett et al.,
1985a,b; McEwen and Mendelson, 1993; Ohi et al.,
1989). In the present study, we used the visible burrow
system (VBS) model to examine the effects of chronic
social stress on the morphology of hippocampal pyra-
midal cells and on the binding to the 5HTT. The VBS
provides a useful model of chronic social stress in a
relatively
natural
setting.
Mixed-sex
rat
colonies
housed in the VBS quickly develop a dominance hier-
archy, which leads to profound behavioral, physiologi-
cal, and endocrine changes in the subordinate males
(Blanchard and Blanchard, 1990; Blanchard et al.,
1993, 1995). Alterations in various neurotransmitter
systems are also seen in colony subordinates (Albeck et
al., 1996; Blanchard et al., 1991; McKittrick et al.,
1995; Watanabe et al., 1995).
Our results concur with previous studies indicating
that, compared to pair-housed controls, the dominant
animals are stressed as well (Blanchard et al., 1993,
1995). Yet despite indices of chronic hypothalamic-
pituitary-adrenal (HPA) activation, the dominant ani-
mals do not show the behavioral and physiological def-
icits apparent in the subordinates, suggesting that
they have successfully adapted to their living condi-
tions. In the present study, remodeling of pyramidal
cell dendritic trees and down-regulation of 5HTT bind-
ing sites within the hippocampus were found in both
the subordinates and the dominants, suggesting that
these changes may be part of the normal adaptive
response to chronic stress.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Several “colonies” consisting of four or ﬁve male and
two female adult rats (99–132 days of age; 350–450 g),
from a Long-Evans strain maintained at the University
of Hawaii, were housed in a Visible Burrow System
(VBS). The VBS apparatus and protocol have been
described in detail elsewhere (Blanchard and Blan-
chard, 1990). Brieﬂy, the VBS consists of a large open
ﬁeld area (86 3 65 cm) connected to several smaller
chambers by a series of tunnels. Food and water were
available in the open ﬁeld ad libitum and this area
received light from a single 15W bulb on a 12:12 light:
dark cycle, while the burrows and tunnels were kept
dark. The animals in the VBS were videotaped period-
ically throughout the experiment for subsequent be-
havioral analysis. The dominant/subordinate status of
each animal was determined by analysis of offensive
and defensive behaviors, wound number and pattern,
and changes in body weight. Females served to poten-
tiate male territoriality and dominance hierarchy for-
mation although no data were collected on them. Con-
trols were age- and weight-matched males housed in
male-female pairs kept on the same light:dark cycle in
conventional cages with food and water available ad
libitum.
Ten colonies and 12 controls were used in Experi-
ment 1. On day 14, males were quickly removed from
the VBS or control cages in the late morning (between
10:00 and 12:00) and placed in a restrainer and tail
blood was collected within 3 minutes for measurement
of VBS effects on corticosterone (CORT). After sam-
pling, animals were returned to their VBS or control
cages. The following day, males were sacriﬁced by de-
capitation and the brains and peripheral organs were
quickly removed, frozen on powdered dry ice, and
stored at 270°C. The brains were processed for 5HTT
autoradiography as described below.
Eight colonies and eight controls were used in Ex-
periment 2, which was conducted at a later date. In
contrast to Experiment 1, in the late morning of day 14,
animals were removed from the VBS and allowed to
recover in individual cages for 1 hour prior to blood
sampling in order to measure basal CORT outside the
context of the VBS. Control males were separated from
females for 1 hour prior to sampling. The next day,
males were deeply anesthetized and transcardially per-
fused with 4% paraformaldehyde in 0.1 M phosphate
buffer containing 1.5% (v/v) picric acid. The ﬁxed
brains were processed for Golgi impregnation as de-
scribed below.
Corticosterone Measurement
Total CORT was measured in plasma samples from
Experiments 1 and 2. Plasma was diluted 1:50 in 0.01
M PBS and heated for 1 hour at 60°C to inactivate
corticosteroid binding globulins. CORT levels were de-
termined by radioimmunoassay using rabbit anti-
86
C.R. MCKITTRICK ET AL.

two female adult rats (99–132 days of age; 350–450 g),
from a Long-Evans strain maintained at the University
of Hawaii, were housed in a Visible Burrow System
(VBS). The VBS apparatus and protocol have been
described in detail elsewhere (Blanchard and Blan-
chard, 1990). Brieﬂy, the VBS consists of a large open
ﬁeld area (86 3 65 cm) connected to several smaller
chambers by a series of tunnels. Food and water were
available in the open ﬁeld ad libitum and this area
received light from a single 15W bulb on a 12:12 light:
dark cycle, while the burrows and tunnels were kept
dark. The animals in the VBS were videotaped period-
ically throughout the experiment for subsequent be-
havioral analysis. The dominant/subordinate status of
each animal was determined by analysis of offensive
and defensive behaviors, wound number and pattern,
and changes in body weight. Females served to poten-
tiate male territoriality and dominance hierarchy for-
mation although no data were collected on them. Con-
trols were age- and weight-matched males housed in
male-female pairs kept on the same light:dark cycle in
conventional cages with food and water available ad
Ten colonies and 12 controls were used in Experi-
ment 1. On day 14, males were quickly removed from
the VBS or control cages in the late morning (between
10:00 and 12:00) and placed in a restrainer and tail
blood was collected within 3 minutes for measurement
of VBS effects on corticosterone (CORT). After sam-
pling, animals were returned to their VBS or control
cages. The following day, males were sacriﬁced by de-
capitation and the brains and peripheral organs were
quickly removed, frozen on powdered dry ice, and
stored at 270°C. The brains were processed for 5HTT
autoradiography as described below.
Eight colonies and eight controls were used in Ex-
periment 2, which was conducted at a later date. In
contrast to Experiment 1, in the late morning of day 14,
animals were removed from the VBS and allowed to
recover in individual cages for 1 hour prior to blood
sampling in order to measure basal CORT outside the
context of the VBS. Control males were separated from
females for 1 hour prior to sampling. The next day,
males were deeply anesthetized and transcardially per-
fused with 4% paraformaldehyde in 0.1 M phosphate
buffer containing 1.5% (v/v) picric acid. The ﬁxed
brains were processed for Golgi impregnation as de-
Total CORT was measured in plasma samples from
Experiments 1 and 2. Plasma was diluted 1:50 in 0.01
M PBS and heated for 1 hour at 60°C to inactivate
corticosteroid binding globulins. CORT levels were de-
termined by radioimmunoassay using rabbit anti-
serum raised against corticosterone-21-hemisuccinate
BSAS (B21-42; Endocrine Sciences, Calabasas, CA) as
described previously (Spencer et al., 1996). Sensitivity
of the assay was 10 pg and the intraassay coefﬁcient of
After sacriﬁce, brains were postﬁxed in perfusate
overnight, then cut into 100-mm sections on a Vi-
bratome into a bath of 3% potassium dichromate in
distilled water. Sections were processed using a modi-
ﬁed single-section Golgi impregnation procedure (Gab-
bott and Somogyi, 1984). Brieﬂy, brain sections were
incubated overnight in 3% potassium dichromate,
rinsed in distilled water, and mounted on plain glass
slides. A coverslip was glued over the sections at the
four corners and these slide assemblies were incubated
overnight in 1.5% silver nitrate in the dark. The fol-
lowing day, the slide assemblies were dismantled and
the tissue sections were rinsed in distilled water and
dehydrated in a series of graded alcohols, and cleared
with Histoclear (Americlear). Finally, sections were
mounted onto gelatin-coated slides and coverslipped
with Permount (Fisher Scientiﬁc, Springﬁeld, NJ).
Slides were coded prior to quantitative
analysis; the code was not broken until analysis was
complete. From each brain, 6–8 pyramidal cells that
met the following criteria were selected for analysis: (1)
location within the CA3 subﬁeld of the dorsal hip-
throughout the extent of all dendrites, (3) relative iso-
lation from neighboring impregnated cells, and (4) cell
bodies in the middle third of the tissue section. Each
neuron was traced at 4003 magniﬁcation using a light
microscope with a camera lucida drawing tube attach-
ment. From these drawings, the number of dendritic
branch (bifurcation) points within a 100-mm-thick sec-
tion of each dendritic tree was determined for each
selected neuron. In addition, the length of the den-
drites present in a 100-mm-thick section was measured
using a Zeiss Interactive Digitizing Analysis System
(ZIDAS). Mean number of branch points and mean
dendritic length were calculated for each brain and
statistical analysis was performed as described below.
Coronal sections (16 mm) through dorsal hippocam-
pus and dorsal and median raphe nuclei were pro-
cessed for 5HTT autoradiography as described by Men-
delson et al. (1993). Slides were preincubated for 15
minutes at room temperature in 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH
7.7), then incubated 2 hours at room temperature in 50
mM Tris-HCl, 120 mM NaCl, 5 mM KCl, and 0.4 nM
[3H]paroxetine (NEN, Boston, MA: speciﬁc activity 5
22 Ci/mmol). Nonspeciﬁc binding was determined in
the presence of 40 mM ﬂuoxetine hydrochloride (a gift
from Eli Lilly, Indianapolis, IN). Slides were washed
twice for 45 minutes at room temperature in incubation
buffer without ligand, dipped in 4°C double distilled
water and air-dried. Radiolabeled sections were ap-
posed to 3Hyperﬁlm (Amersham, Arlington Heights,
IL) at room temperature and developed after 21 days.
Autoradiograms were analyzed by com-
puter-assisted microdensitometry (Imaging Research,
St. Catharine’s, Ontario, Canada) with optical densi-
ties converted into moles radioligand bound per mg
tissue (wet weight) using curves generated from triti-
ated microscale standards (Amersham) coexposed with
the labeled sections. Total binding was measured in six
sections from each animal and these measurements
were averaged for each brain region. Nonspeciﬁc bind-
ing measured on alternate sections was subtracted
from total binding to yield mean speciﬁc binding for
each region in each animal; statistical analyses of these
values were performed as described below.
Statistical analysis was done using one-way analysis
of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey’s post-hoc test
when indicated. A level of P # 0.05 was considered
Housing in the VBS led to an elevation of plasma
CORT in dominant and subordinate animals compared
to controls when sampled immediately after removal
from the VBS (Table I), suggesting that the VBS envi-
ronment is stressful for both groups of animals
(F(2,53) 5 4.933, P 5 0.011; DOM vs. CON, P 5 0.018;
SUB vs. CON, P 5 0.023). However, if animals were
allowed to rest 1 hour prior to sampling, plasma CORT
fell to levels comparable to controls in dominants while
remaining elevated in subordinates (F(2,25) 5 6.652,
P 5 0.005; SUB vs. CON, P 5 0.011, SUB vs. DOM, P 5
TABLE I. Plasma corticosterone levels within VBS and 1 hour after
1All values are expressed as group mean 6 SEM. Numbers of animals per group
are in parentheses. *Different from control, P , 0.05; †different from dominant,
2After 14 days of mixed-sex group housing, tail blood was collected from males
within 3 min of removal from the VBS or control cages (Expt. 1).
3Tail blood was collected from males after animals had been allowed to rest
quietly in individual cages for 1 hour after removal from the VBS or control cages
CHRONIC STRESS, DENDRITIC MORPHOLOGY, AND SEROTONIN
Initial and ﬁnal body weights and ﬁnal organ
weights of VBS animals and controls from Experiment
1 are shown in Table II. Subordinate animals lost over
20% of their original body weight during the 14 days of
VBS housing, in contrast to both controls and domi-
nants, which showed little or no change (%StartWt:
F(2,50) 5 112.08, P , 0.001; DOM vs. CON, P 5 0.003;
SUB vs. CON, DOM, P , 0.001). Similar results were
obtained in Experiment 2 (data not shown). However,
dominant animals had increased adrenal weights and
decreased thymus weights compared to controls; simi-
lar effects were seen in subordinates, with thymus
weights of subordinates differing from dominants as
well (Adrenals: F(2,50) 5 8.147, P 5 0.001; DOM vs.
CON, P 5 0.003; SUB vs. CON, P 5 0.002; Thymus:
F(2,49) 5 97.97, P , 0.001; DOM vs. CON, P , 0.001;
SUB vs. CON, DOM, P , 0.001). Spleen weights were
unaffected (F(2,50) 5 1.473, P 5 0.239). The results of
the statistical comparisons are unchanged if adrenal
and thymus weights are expressed as a proportion of
ﬁnal body weight (data not shown). When spleen
weight is expressed as a proportion of ﬁnal body
weight, the adjusted spleen weight is signiﬁcantly
larger in subordinates compared to controls (F(2, 50) 5
4.603, P 5 0.015; SUB vs. CON, P 5 0.011); this result
should be interpreted with caution, however, since the
adjusted spleen weight may be skewed by the profound
and rapid loss in body weight observed in the subordi-
Quantitative analysis of Golgi-impregnated cells in-
dicates that group-housing in the VBS environment
leads to morphological changes in hippocampal pyra-
midal neurons of both dominant and subordinate ani-
mals. Both dominant and subordinate animals had a
decrease in the mean number of dendritic branch
points (Fig. 1A; F(2,26) 5 5.343, P 5 0.011; DOM vs.
CON, P 5 0.009; SUB vs. CON, P 5 0.045). In addition,
the total length of apical dendrites was decreased in
the dominant animals (Fig. 1B; F(2,26) 5 3.783, P 5
0.036; DOM vs. CON, P 5 0.028). There were no dif-
ferences among groups in either parameter in the basal
dendritic trees (branch points: F(2,26) 5 1.532, P 5
0.235; dendritic length: F(2,26) 5 1.464, P 5 0.250). The
difference in arborization of the apical dendritic trees is
illustrated in camera lucida drawings of representative
neurons from each group (Fig. 2).
5HTT binding in the brains of control and VBS-
housed animals was measured using quantitative re-
ceptor autoradiography with [3H]paroxetine. Repre-
sentative autoradiograms of [3H]paroxetine binding to
sections taken through dorsal hippocampus and the
raphe nuclei are shown in Figure 3. 5HTT binding in
TABLE II. Body and organ weights after 14 days of VBS housing1
1All values are expressed as group mean 6 SEM. Numbers of animals per group are in parentheses. Final weight as a percentage of starting weight was calcu-
lated for each individual and these values were used for statistical comparisons. *Different from control, P , 0.05; †different from dominant, P , 0.05.
Effects of chronic social stress on the number of dendritic
branch points (A) and total dendritic length (B) of CA3 pyramidal
neurons in hippocampus. Dominant and subordinate animals were
housed in mixed-sex groups in a VBS for 14 days with rank deter-
mined as described in Materials and Methods, while control males
were housed in male-female pairs in conventional cages. Data in
A and B are expressed as mean 6 SEM; *signiﬁcantly different
from Control, P , 0.05. Control: n 5 5; Dominant: n 5 7; Subordinate:
VBS animals was signiﬁcantly decreased compared to
controls in the pyramidal cell layer and radiatum/lacu-
nosum moleculare layers of Ammon’s horn (Fig. 4A;
CA1-oriens: F(2,54) 5 1.043, P 5 0.359; CA1-pyr:
F(2,54) 5 1.340, P 5 0.270: CA1-rlm: F(2,54) 5 4.184, P 5
0.020; DOM vs. CON, P 5 0.015; CA2-oriens: F(2,54) 5
3.212, P 5 0.048; posthoc comparisons not signiﬁcant;
CA2-pyr: F(2,54) 5 4.688, P 5 0.013; DOM vs. CON, P 5
0.015; SUB vs. CON, P 5 0.040; CA2-rlm: F(2,54) 5
2.710, P 5 0.076; CA3-oriens: F(2,54) 5 3.025, P 5 0.057;
CA3-pyr: F(2,54) 5 3.034, P 5 0.056; DOM vs. CON, P 5
0.045; CA3-rlm: F(2,54) 5 4.011, P 5 0.024; DOM vs.
CON, P 5 0.036; SUB vs. CON, P 5 0.042; CA4-oriens:
F(2,54) 5 3.124, P 5 0.052; CA4-pyr: F(2,54) 5 3.390, P 5
0.041; DOM vs. CON, P 5 0.034; CA4-rlm: F(2,54) 5
4.073, P 5 0.023; DOM vs. CON, P 5 0.019). These
effects were most pronounced in the dominant animals,
which had signiﬁcant decreases in the pyramidal layer
of CA2, CA3, and CA4 and the radiatum/l.moleculare
layers of CA1, CA3. and CA4. Binding was also some-
what decreased in these regions in the subordinate
animals although the difference from control was sig-
niﬁcant only in the pyramidal cell layer of CA2 and
radiatum/l.moleculare of CA3. In contrast, there were
no differences among groups in 5HTT binding density
in dentate gyrus (Fig. 4B; suprapyramidal blade:
F(2,54) 5 0.441, P 5 0.646; infrapyramidal blade:
F(2,54) 5 0.325, P 5 0.724) or in dorsal or median raphe
(Fig. 4C; dorsal: F(2,53) 5 0.146, P 5 0.864; median:
The visible burrow system (VBS) is a unique model of
chronic stress: the formation of a dominance hierarchy
among the males leads to a stressful environment that
is continuous throughout the duration of the experi-
ment, but is somewhat variable due to unstructured
interactions among the animals, and has the distinct
advantage of involving little experimenter manipula-
tion. In contrast, other chronic stress paradigms usu-
ally involve repeated application of the same stressor,
often at the same time of day, such that the stressor
administration becomes predictable, thus facilitating
physiological habituation and adaptation (see Blan-
chard et al., 1998b, for a detailed comparison of the
VBS model with other chronic stress models). The na-
ture of the stress within the VBS is more psychological
than physical since the level of ﬁghting shows a
marked decline after establishment of the hierarchy
within the ﬁrst day or two of group housing (Blanchard
et al., 1995). Among the VBS animals, subordinates
show the most pronounced consequences of stress, in-
cluding dramatic weight loss and early mortality, as
well as decreased aggression, copulation, feeding, and
activity (Blanchard and Blanchard, 1990). There ap-
pears to be no habituation in this stress paradigm,
Camera lucida drawings of representative Golgi-impreg-
nated CA3 pyramidal neurons in male rats housed in male-female
pairs in conventional cages (Control) or in mixed-sex groups in a VBS
(Dominant and Subordinate). Note the decreased complexity of the
apical dendritic trees (top) in the animals housed in the VBS, partic-
CHRONIC STRESS, DENDRITIC MORPHOLOGY, AND SEROTONIN
since these effects continue to worsen with time spent
in the VBS (Blanchard and Blanchard, 1990; Blan-
chard et al.,1993). The subordinate animals also ex-
hibit several stress-related endocrine changes, includ-
downregulated Type I and Type II glucocorticoid recep-
tor gene expression, and inhibited testosterone secre-
tion (Blanchard et al., 1993, 1995; Chao et al., 1993).
Examination of non-neural indices of hypothalamic-
pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis activation suggests that,
compared to controls, the dominant animals are also
stressed. In the present study, dominant animals, like
the subordinates, show moderate elevations of cortico-
sterone within the VBS as well as adrenal hypertrophy
and thymus involution; these results concur with data
from previous VBS studies (Blanchard et al., 1993,
1995). Feedback inhibition of the HPA axis appears to
be more efﬁcient in dominants compared to subordi-
nates, however, since CORT levels in dominants are
not different from controls when measured 1 hour after
removal from the VBS. Neurochemical studies using
the VBS model have found other stress-related alter-
ations common to both the dominants and subordi-
nates, including decreased 5HT1A receptor binding in
hippocampus (McKittrick et al., 1995) and increased
levels of CRF mRNA in hypothalamus (Albeck et al.,
changes that occur in hippocampus of both dominants
and subordinates. A signiﬁcant shrinkage of the apical
dendritic arbors of hippocampal CA3 pyramidal neu-
rons is seen in all animals in the VBS. There is a
decrease in arbor complexity (branch points) in both
dominants and subordinates while dominants have a
reduction in total dendritic length as well. A study of
tree shrews has shown similar dendritic atrophy in
subordinates (Magarin˜os et al., 1996), but pyramidal
cell morphology in dominant animals has never before
Typical autoradiograms of [3H]
paroxetine binding to the 5HTT in sections
take through dorsal hippocampus (top) and
the midbrain raphe nuclei (bottom). Binding
was measured using computer-assisted mi-
crodensitometry in the following regions:
CA1, CA2, CA3, and CA4 of Ammon’s horn,
dentate gyrus (DG), dorsal raphe nucleus
(DR), and median raphe nucleus (MR).
been examined. The observation that these changes
occur to a similar (or greater) extent in dominants as
well as in the more severely stressed subordinates sug-
gests that dendritic remodeling may be a common re-
sponse to chronic activation of the HPA axis but does
not vary signiﬁcantly with the severity of the stress.
This conclusion is supported by data showing similar
degrees of dendritic atrophy in animals subjected ei-
ther to the relatively mild stressor of repeated restraint
or to a more severe chronic variable stress regimen
(Magarin˜os and McEwen, 1995b).
In addition to the morphological changes, there is
decreased binding to the 5HT transporter throughout
Ammon’s horn in animals housed in the VBS, with the
greatest effects seen in the dominants. There are, how-
ever, no changes in 5HTT binding in the dentate gyrus
or in the raphe nuclei. To our knowledge, this is the
ﬁrst demonstration of regulation of the hippocampal
5HT transporter by chronic stress. Down-regulation of
5HTT binding has been observed in cortical mem-
branes following 1 or 2 weeks of food restriction, but
binding to hippocampal membranes was not examined
(Huether et al., 1997). Although subordinate animals
in our model did show signiﬁcant weight loss as a
result of VBS housing (most likely due to a combination
of reduced food intake and metabolic changes; see
Blanchard et al.,1998a), it is unlikely that reduced
caloric intake played a major role in the changes we
observed, since no weight loss occurred in the domi-
nants. Conversely, the down-regulation observed in the
Huether study could be attributed to the stressful na-
The decreases in 5HTT binding and any associated
changes in 5HT reuptake may be related to the den-
dritic remodeling seen in the VBS animals. Within the
hippocampus, 5HTT binding density is highest in CA3,
suggesting that this region receives signiﬁcant seroto-
nergic input. Anatomically, the strata where 5HTT
binding is decreased (pyramidale, radiatum, and lacu-
nosum moleculare) correspond to the regions in which
the apical dendritic trees of CA3 pyramidal originate
and to which they extend. Previous studies have shown
blocked by tianeptine (Watanabe et al., 1992b), a drug
that has been shown to reduce extracellular 5HT levels
through enhanced reuptake (Fattaccini et al., 1990;
Effects of chronic social stress on 5HTT binding in Am-
mon’s horn of hippocampus (A), dentate gyrus (B), and raphe nuclei
(C). Control males were housed in male-female pairs in conventional
cages for 14 days. Binding densities are expressed as fmol [3H]parox-
etine speciﬁcally bound per mg tissue equivalent. Measurements were
taken in stratum oriens (oriens), stratum pyramidale (pyr), and com-
bined radiatum/lacunosum moleculare (rlm) of the CA ﬁelds; in the
suprapyramidal and infrapyramidal blades of dentate gyrus; and in
dorsal and median raphe. Data are expressed as mean 6 SEM; *sig-
niﬁcantly different from Control, P , 0.05. Control: n 5 12; Dominant:
CHRONIC STRESS, DENDRITIC MORPHOLOGY, AND SEROTONIN
Mennini et al., 1987; Whitton et al., 1991), suggesting
a role for 5HT in the mechanisms mediating the re-
modeling. The down-regulation of 5HT transporters
may have the opposite effect of tianeptine by decreas-
ing the rate of clearance of synaptically released 5HT,
thus facilitating serotonergic neurotransmission. It is
interesting to note that, in contrast to the results from
the 14-day VBS protocol, 14 days of repeated restraint
had no effect on 5HTT binding (Watanabe et al.,1993);
this stress regimen was also not sufﬁcient for the in-
duction of morphological changes in CA3 (Magarin˜os
Differential regulation of 5HT receptor subtypes by
stress, in addition to changes in 5HT transporters, may
also alter 5HT neurotransmission in hippocampus,
leading to changes in dendritic morphology. It has pre-
viously been shown that 5HT1A receptors are reduced
in hippocampus of both dominants and subordinates in
the VBS, while hippocampal 5HT2A receptor binding is
unchanged (McKittrick et al., 1995). There is some
evidence to suggest that, at least during development,
activation of 5HT1A receptors plays a role in the devel-
opment and maintenance of the dendritic trees of sep-
tal and hippocampal neurons (Riad et al., 1994; Yan et
al., 1997); these 5HT1A-mediated trophic effects may be
reduced in the VBS animals, leading to the observed
shrinkage of the apical dendritic trees in CA3 pyrami-
Alternatively, morphology may be affected by 5HT-
receptor modulation of excitatory neurotransmission.
Prior studies have demonstrated that glutamate, act-
ing via the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor
site, is critically involved in the mechanisms underly-
ing stress-induced atrophy (Magarin˜os and McEwen,
1995a; Magarin˜os et al., 1996; Watanabe et al., 1992a).
Glutamate- and NMDA-mediated excitatory responses
have, in turn, been shown to be enhanced by concur-
rent activation of 5HT2A receptors (Rahman and Neu-
man, 1993; Trueblood et al., 1996). The altered balance
of 5HT receptors in the hippocampus of VBS animals
may shift the net activity of synaptically released 5HT
away from 5HT1A-mediated inhibitory effects and to-
ward 5HT2A-facilitated excitation. At the same time,
these postsynaptic effects may be prolonged by de-
creased reuptake through the 5HT transporter (see
above), in conjunction with stress-induced increases in
presynaptic release of both 5HT (Joseph and Kennett,
1983; Pei et al., 1990; Vahabzadeh and Fillenz, 1994)
and glutamate (Lowy et al., 1995; Moghaddam, 1993).
Thus, stress-induced dendritic remodeling may involve
complex interactions between serotonergic and excita-
tory amino acid transmitter systems within the hip-
The signiﬁcance of the morphological and neuro-
chemical changes in the VBS animals is unclear. The
morphological restructuring of CA3 pyramidal cell den-
dritic trees may be part of the normal adaptive re-
sponse to stress, rather than the ﬁrst step in an irre-
vocable cascade leading to pyramidal cell death.
Several observations support the hypothesis that den-
dritic remodeling does not necessarily represent a
pathological response to stress. First, dendritic atrophy
due to stress and glucocorticoids occurs within a matter
of weeks (Magarin˜os and McEwen, 1995a,b; Watanabe
et al., 1992c; Woolley et al., 1990), whereas somatic
damage and death of pyramidal neurons is seen only
after months of exposure to high levels of glucocorti-
coids (Sapolsky et al., 1985, 1990; Uno et al., 1989) or in
the presence of a concurrent insult like ischemia or
seizure activity (reviewed in Sapolsky, 1990). Second,
dendritic arborization returns to normal after termina-
tion of the stressor indicating that pyramidal cell den-
drites retain plasticity under conditions that induce
remodeling (Conrad et al., 1999). Finally, the observa-
tion that the shrinkage of dendritic arbors is somewhat
more pronounced in the dominant animals suggests a
dissociation between the morphological changes and
the more deleterious stress-related deﬁcits seen in the
subordinates such as abnormal behavior, early mortal-
ity, and gonadal hormone suppression (Blanchard et
The hippocampal formation and the CA3 subregion
in particular have been implicated in the integration
and consolidation of information underlying associa-
tive memory (Eichenbaum et al., 1992; Wiebe et al.,
1997) and it is likely that these functions are altered by
chronic stress. It is possible that dendritic atrophy may
be associated with deﬁcits in spatial learning, but this
hypothesis would be very difﬁcult to test in this model,
due to the decrease in overall locomotor activity in the
subordinates and possible differences in motivation for
reward among the different groups of animals (Blan-
chard and Blanchard, 1990; Yudko et al., 1996). In
those studies where stress regimens known to produce
dendritic remodeling did lead to changes in spatial
memory, the impairments were relatively minor and it
was not clear whether remodeling alone was sufﬁcient
to lead to the deﬁcits (Conrad et al., 1996; Luine et al.,
On the other hand, the shrinkage of the dendritic
trees seen with stress could potentially serve an adap-
tive function by limiting input to the CA3 pyramidal
neurons, thus ﬁltering out extraneous information dur-
ing times of stress. In addition, we have found that
chronic stress in rats leads to reorganization of the
synaptic apparatus within the dentate gyrus mossy
ﬁber terminals that provide excitatory input to CA3
pyramidal neurons (Magarin˜os et al., 1997). The rear-
rangement of clear small vesicles near synaptic active
zones is accompanied by mitochondrial hypertrophy,
suggesting that, after chronic stress, the mossy ﬁber
terminals have increased excitatory output. Thus, the
potentiated glutamate release seen in hippocampus
with acute stress (Lowy et al., 1993; Moghaddam,
1993) may be further enhanced following chronic
stress. Reduction of the postsynaptic surface area of
the distal dendritic tree may be a regulatory mecha-
nism designed to counteract the potentially toxic ef-
fects of exacerbated glutamate input to proximal sites.
Such possible beneﬁts of remodeling may occur in con-
junction with slight decrements in spatial learning.
Although glucocorticoids have been shown to play a
critical role in stress-induced dendritic changes (Ma-
garin˜os and McEwen, 1995a), the role of adrenal ste-
roids in the downregulation of 5HTT binding is cur-
rently unclear. Studies of adrenalectomized (ADX)
animals with Type I or Type II glucocorticoid receptor
agonist replacement showed no effects of any manipu-
lation on 5HTT mRNA in dorsal raphe (Kuroda et al.,
1994); however, mRNA levels were not measured in
median raphe, which provides the majority of 5HT
innervation to the hippocampus (Kellar et al.1977;
Moore and Halaris, 1975; Van de Kar and Lorens,
1979). Corticosterone administration decreased 5HTT
binding in frontal cortex and hypothalamus in both
intact and ADX rats (Arora and Meltzer, 1986), while
chronic dexamethasone led to a decrease in 5HTT
mRNA in midbrain homogenates and decreased synap-
tosomal uptake of [3H]5HT (Fumagalli et al., 1996;
Slotkin et al.1996). 5HTT binding in cortical or hip-
pocampal membranes was unchanged by dexametha-
sone, although it is possible that the subtle changes we
observed in select regions of Ammon’s horn were not
detectable using membranes from tissue homogenates.
Alternatively, 5HTT levels may be regulated by factors
In conclusion, formation and maintenance of a dom-
inance hierarchy among males housed in mixed-sex
groups is stressful for the subordinates and, to a lesser
extent, for the dominants as well. Animals of both
ranks show HPA axis activation within the VBS and
stress-related changes in peripheral organs. In addi-
tion, 5HT transporters are down-regulated in the hip-
pocampus of VBS animals compared to controls, which
may lead to altered 5HT levels within the hippocam-
pus. The apical dendritic trees of CA3 pyramidal neu-
rons are also smaller and less complex in both domi-
nants and subordinates. Increased levels of circulating
glucocorticoids, together with facilitated 5HT and glu-
tamatergic activity, may lead to these morphological
changes. In addition, these neurochemical and morpho-
logical changes appear to be independent of the behav-
ioral and endocrine deﬁcits seen in the subordinate
